entry.title: Saudi Arabia: GOSI Maternity Compensation – Why the “Obvious” Solution Doesn’t Work
entry.content:
Let’s Start with the Questions Everyone Is Asking

When the General Organization for Social Insurance (GOSI) introduced Maternity Compensation, two immediate questions come up in every discussion:

“If GOSI pays the employee… why don’t we just stop salary or reverse it?”
“And why is GOSI paying the employee directly, not the employer?”

Both questions are valid—and actually connected.

Why Does GOSI Pay the Employee Directly?

From a business perspective, many expect the payment to go to the employer first.
But the design from GOSI is intentional:

  • The benefit is meant to support the employee directly
  • It ensures the employee receives compensation without dependency on employer processing
  • It reduces the employer’s burden, but does not replace employer payroll processes automatically

Which creates the core challenge:

"Payroll still pays salary… while GOSI also pays the employee separately."

 

The Real Scenario Everyone Is Facing

Let’s look at what actually happens in companies:

  • An employee takes paid maternity leave, then after a few months receives compensation from GOSI.
Now the employer asks:

“How do I recover the salary I already paid?”

 

First Thought: “Let’s Just Reverse It”

Idea:

Replace paid maternity leave with unpaid leave (retroactively)

Why it sounds correct:
  • Aligns with the legal concept
  • Removes employer cost
  • Clean from a business perspective
Why it doesn’t really work:
  • SAP payroll will reopen past periods
  • Everything gets recalculated (even closed payroll)
  • Financial postings may change
  • Other wage types get impacted (not just maternity-related)

✔️So yes, it solves the business logic…
 ❎But breaks payroll stability

 

Second Thought: “Ok, Then Just Deduct It Later”

Idea:

Recover the amount through future payroll

Why we like it:
  • No retroactive impact
  • Keeps historical payroll unchanged
  • Easy to control
But here’s the catch:
  • We don’t know the exact GOSI amount
  • No clear rule on:
  • Basic vs Housing split

✔️So yes, it solves the technical issue…
❎But doesn’t solve the calculation ambiguity

 

Third Thought: “Can We Automate It Fully?”

Idea:

Build logic in payroll to calculate and distribute everything automatically

Why it’s attractive:
  • No manual work
  • Fully system-driven
  • Clean process
Why we didn’t go there (yet):
  • No confirmed formula from GOSI
-->To elaborate:

GOSI defines the compensation as 100% of the average insured salary over the last 12 months.
However, from a system perspective, this introduces multiple limitations:

  • The 12-month average may include periods where the employee worked for different employers
  • The current employer does not have visibility into salary or contribution data from previous companies
  • There is no integration/API providing this cross-employer historical data
  • Additionally, due to Personal Data Protection Law (PDPL) considerations, the employer does not have the authority to request or rely on the exact compensation amount received by the employee from GOSI
-->Which means:
Even if we automate the calculation in SAP, it would be based on incomplete or indirect data, and cannot be validated against the actual amount paid by GOSI
  • No integration/API to validate results
  • High risk of calculating the wrong amount

✔️So yes, it solves the automation need…
❎But risks compliance and accuracy

 

Fourth Thought: “What If We Handle It Correctly from the Start?”

Idea:

If the employee requests GOSI compensation during the same period, we directly record:

--> Unpaid maternity leave instead of paid leave

Why this is the best-case scenario:
  • No recovery required later
  • No retroactive changes
  • Payroll remains clean
  • Aligns with the GOSI concept
So… what’s still missing?
  • No visibility on actual GOSI compensation amount
  • No defined split between:
    • Basic Salary
    • Housing Allowance
  • Requires strong coordination:
    • HR
    • Payroll
    • Employee
  • Additionally, the employee may dispute the employer’s action based on Saudi Labor Law Article 151, especially if the expectation is to receive paid maternity leave from the employer

✔️ So yes, it solves the timing issue…
❎But introduces potential employee relations and legal interpretation risks, in addition to dependency on external clarity and internal discipline

 

So, What Did We Actually Do?

We stepped back and asked:

“What is the safest way to handle this—without breaking payroll or assuming unclear rules?”

--> That’s why we delivered this as a KBA (Knowledge-Based Approach)

 

Reference: SAP KBA

For detailed configuration steps and system behavior, please refer to the official SAP Knowledge Base Article: 🔗KBA 

This KBA provides:
  • Step-by-step implementation guidance
  • Detailed explanation of standard payroll behavior
  • Recommended approaches (IT0014, IT0045, IT2001)
  • Technical considerations for retroactivity and deductions

 

Why a KBA Instead of a Standard Solution?

Because:

  • GOSI doesn’t provide full calculation details
  • SAP doesn’t receive actual compensation values
  • Customers handle cases differently
  • Each scenario depends on timing and business decisions

--> A fixed solution would either:

  • Be too rigid
  • Or introduce incorrect calculations

--> A KBA provides:

  • Flexibility
  • Control
  • Safe implementation

 

Let’s Be Honest About the Options

Let’s look at each option realistically:

  • Unpaid Leave (Same Period)
    This is the cleanest approach from a process perspective, as it avoids any recovery later and keeps payroll aligned from the beginning. However, it depends heavily on early action and still suffers from unclear calculation rules.
  • Unpaid Leave (Retroactive)
    This can correct past payments and align with the legal concept, but it comes at a high cost—triggering retroactive payroll recalculations and impacting payroll stability.
  • Recurring Deduction (IT0014)
    A safe and practical method to recover the amount in future payroll periods. It is simple and controlled but relies on manual input since the exact calculation from GOSI is not available.
  • Loan (IT0045)
    Provides structured tracking and visibility of the remaining balance, making it suitable for financial control. However, it is more complex to manage and still depends on manual calculation.
  • Automation (PCR)
    This approach offers full automation and flexibility in distributing amounts across wage components. However, without a reliable calculation source from GOSI, it introduces a high risk of incorrect results.

 

The Hidden Problem: Net Pay & Arrears

Even with the best approach, another issue appears:

“What if the deduction is higher than the employee’s net salary?”

--> SAP will:

  • Deduct partially
  • Carry the remaining amount as arrears (claims)
  • Recover it in future payroll periods

--> Meaning:

  • Recovery becomes gradual
  • Not guaranteed in one cycle

 

The Big Missing Piece

Everything comes back to one point:

We still don’t have a clear answer from GOSI on:

  • Exact calculation method
  • Component distribution
  • Actual compensation value

 

So, What’s the Right Approach Today?

Not perfect but practical:

✅Handle correctly from the start when possible
✅Avoid retroactive payroll changes
✅Use forward recovery when needed
✅Keep logic flexible and controlled

 

Final Thought

This is one of those cases where:

The legal idea is simple…
But system reality is not.

Every solution we explored:

  • Solves one part of the problem
  • But not the full picture

--> That’s why flexibility not automation is the right approach for now

 

What We’re Waiting For

To move toward a standard solution, we need:

  • Clear calculation rules from GOSI
  • Integration or API availability
  • Standardized scenarios

 

Stay Updated

To stay informed about upcoming updates, enhancements, and discussions related to this topic, we recommend visiting the MENA SAP User Group space, where we will be sharing the latest developments and guidance.

entry.title: Re: SAC setVisible via Switch
entry.content:
So This is how I set it up.

Added a switch and 2 tables. I have not overlapped the tables just to show it clearly.

Kept the switch to off initially. Then manually hid the table in left outline panel which I want to hide when switch is off. In My case Table 1.

So Initial view is showing Table 2 and switch off.

Script on change event of the switch.

if (Switch_1.isOn()) 
{
		Table_1.setVisible(true);
        Table_2.setVisible(false);
    } else {
        Table_1.setVisible(false);
		Table_2.setVisible(true);
	}

This is how it works.

Switch off, Table 2 is visible as set initially.

When switch is set to on, Table 1 is visible and Table 2 is hidden.

If you overlap the table on each other you will get the effect of a single table changing view.

Hope this helps !

Nikhil

entry.title: Re: Make deliver note as mandatory when posting in MIGO w...
entry.content:
Dear @Moon3,

The correct enhancement spot for validating Delivery Note in Post Goods Movement (MIGO) is Badi MMIM_HEAD_CHECK_DATA because this is the header-level validation Badi for that app. SAP also explicitly recommends using MMIM_HEAD_CHECK_DATA for validation

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/32da8359c8ee4e8b8e8c5e15cacba5aa/85a220ae29a745aab169b630791d0487.html

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3610497

I have prepared a ready-made code that works:

DATA ls_message TYPE if_mmim_head_check_data=>ts_messages.

IF invtrymgmtcallingappl = 'MIGO'.

  IF header-deliverydocumentbyvendor IS INITIAL.

    ls_message-messagetype = 'E'.

    ls_message-messagetext = 'Enter delivery note'.

    APPEND ls_message TO messages.

  ENDIF.

ENDIF.

HOWEVER this requirement cannot be restricted strictly to movement types 107 and 109 because the released Badi interface available in the system does not expose a movement type field for that header validation. In that case the validation can only be implemented at the overall MIGO / Post Goods Movement level

Below available fields:

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: IAS Proxy System: Is fixed group assignment possible without sending groups in the inbound payload?
entry.content:
IAS Proxy System: Is fixed group assignment possible without sending groups in the inbound payload?

Hi all,

we are working with SAP Identity Provisioning and Identity Authentication as a proxy system. Our client calls the proxy SCIM endpoint like this:

/ipsproxy/service/api/v1/scim/<system_id>/Users

Our user provisioning itself works fine, including custom attributes via proxy read/write transformations.

What we are trying to achieve now is this: A newly created user should automatically be assigned to one fixed group that already exists in IAS.

What makes this a bit confusing is that in standard provisioning from a source system to a target system, SAP documents group assignment via assignGroup, where groups are identified by ID in the target transformation. So for source-to-target provisioning, assigning groups by target group ID seems to be supported.

However, in our case we are not using a normal source-to-target provisioning flow, but an IAS proxy system.

We found SAP KBA 3520458: Provision user's group assignment in Identity Authentication IPS proxy system

The KBA suggests the following mappings:

Read transformation

{
  "sourcePath": "$.groups",
  "targetPath": "$.groups",
  "optional": true,
  "preserveArrayWithSingleElement": true
}

Write transformation

{
  "sourcePath": "$.groups[*].value",
  "targetPath": "$.groups[?(@.value)]",
  "optional": true,
  "preserveArrayWithSingleElement": true
}

Our understanding is:

  • In standard source-to-target provisioning, assignGroup by group ID is supported in the target transformation.
  • In proxy mode, the KBA mapping seems to only pass through groups if they are already present in the inbound payload.
  • That would mean the calling system must send the IAS group ID in groups[*].value.

So our actual question is:

Is it supported in an IAS proxy system to assign a fixed, already existing IAS group purely inside the proxy transformation, even if the inbound payload does not contain groups?

Or is the only supported option in proxy mode one of these two?

  1. The calling system sends the group ID in the user payload.
  2. The user is assigned afterwards via a separate PATCH /Groups/{groupId} call.

Any confirmation, SAP guidance, or working example would be very helpful.

Thanks.

entry.title: A Look Under the Hood: SAP's Accounting Business Transaction Interface (ABTI)
entry.content:
What is ABTI?

The Accounting Business Transaction Interface (ABTI) is the connection point between SAP's Finance Business Transaction Interface (FIBTI) and Accounting. When a business transaction occurs, ABTI is the interface that receives that data and turns it into a real journal entry in Accounting.

FIBTI acts as the coordinator, collecting transaction data from sender applications and passing it along.

ABTI then takes over and processes that data and generates the journal entry in Accounting.

Why was ABTI developed?

A key motivation behind ABTI is decoupling.

First, operational and non‑Finance applications (such as Payroll or Real Estate) are decoupled from Finance‑specific logic. Through FIBTI, sender applications no longer need to deal with Accounting concerns like G/L account determination or valuation and can remain focused on their own business domain.
Second, ABTI introduces decoupling within Finance itself: business transactions received via FIBTI are handed over to Accounting independently, ensuring that Accounting-specific processing is clearly separated and centrally handled.

Previously, sender applications had to handle a lot of Accounting-specific logic themselves – including G/L account determination and currency valuation. ABTI moves all of that responsibility into Accounting, keeping sender applications focused on their own domain.

How does it work – at a high level?

ABTI's processing happens in two main phases:

  • Business transaction data is transformed into a preliminary journal entry. This logic differs depending on the transaction type, because a payroll posting looks very different from a bank fee, for example.
  • The preliminary journal entry is then processed further – G/L accounts are determined, currencies are converted, account assignments are derived, and period and budget checks are performed.

Good to know

Most users won't probably interact with ABTI directly. But knowing it exists, and what it does, can be useful when troubleshooting, configuring, or simply trying to understand how the pieces fit together. Especially because ABTI follows a posting logic that differs significantly from classic postings, it introduces new substitution concepts, and some classic BAdIs and substitutions are not processed in ABTI postings.

You can find more information on ABTI in the SAP Help Portal under Accounting Business Transaction Interface (ABTI) | SAP Help Portal.

And if you are interested in more theory regarding the financial business transaction interface (FIBTI), you can read the Posting Logic of FIBTI: How SAP Finance Transactions Are Orchestrated - SAP Community blog or go to the SAP Help Portal under Finance Business Transaction Interface (FIBTI) | SAP Help Portal.

entry.title: Handling of Round-Off Values in SAP CAR and Integration with S/4HANA
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I’m looking for some clarification on how round-off values from POS transactions are handled within SAP Customer Activity Repository, particularly in scenarios involving invoice-level balancing.

Context:

  1. POS transactions include a round-off amount.
  2. In SAP CAR, I have the balancing of totals task active at the per-invoice level.
  3. This requires that the sum of line item totals matches the tender totals, including any round-off differences, to allow further aggregation.

Questions:

  1. How does SAP Customer Activity Repository process and store round-off values coming from POS?
  2. In cases where invoice-level balancing is enforced, what is the recommended approach to ensure line-item totals align with tender totals when round-off is involved?
  3. How are these round-off values transferred to SAP S/4HANA during financial posting?

Is there any standard configuration or best practice to handle round-off differences consistently across CAR and S/4HANA?
Any insights, configuration pointers, or examples would be greatly appreciated.

entry.title: Re: Create supplier invoice app and import supplier invoi...
entry.content:
Good day @charlene2 

Thank you for your question.

That situation is usually caused by a difference in the source document type, not by the app name itself. In DRC, supplier invoice eDocument creation is controlled by which source document type is created in accounting and whether the source type is activated and mapped correctly for your company code. There are two separate mandatory configuration paths for supplier invoices. These are:

  1. Accounting Document source type: this requires you to activate Source Type Documents for Company Code and Assign eDocument Type to Accounting Document Type.
  2. Invoice Verification source type: this requires assigning eDocument Type to Invoice Verification Transaction. You can use this when you want to create electronic invoices based on supplier invoices from the Create Supplier Invoice-Advanced app.

So, that distinction explains the result you received where:

  • Create Supplier Invoice-Advanced works because it follows the invoice Verification path, and that mapping is already in place.
  • Create Incoming Invoices works because the FI document type you posted is likely already mapped under Assign eDocument Type to Accounting Document Type.
  • Create Supplier Invoice and Import Supplier Invoice creating pure FI supplier invoices do not flow to DRC most likely because the Accounting Document source type or the exact FI document type used by those postings is not activated or not assigned to an eDocument type.

So, if they are not generating eDocuments, the first thing you should check is whether the posted accounting document type from those two apps is assigned and whether Accounting Document is activated.

See the following references for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/8bdd8d920801423ca0f74c026854ebb1/3d515be6943a48b1a62f0013bfa3566f.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/4977e7737cc747edb51d806619e3d146/38be92f5e67d40898db8f91273f7b12d.html?locale=en-US

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: PPDS PTF Upgrade 2023
entry.content:
Hi,

It appears that, following the Hana 2023 Upgrade, system no longer calculates the planiing time fence strictly in working days as it did before, but now sets it according to the delivery schedule calendar set in the Material master. This is also what is displayed in the product view transaction

For example for a material with a time fence of 3 days but delivered monthly according to calendar, the time fence will be set one month later rather than 3 days later.

Before the upgrade, in case of shortage, the heuristic would create element after the 3 days of time fence but would break the calendar rule and create a schedule line anyway before the theoretical delivery date.
Now in a similar case, the heuristic selects the highest value (between calendar and number of time fence days) and puts the first schedule line after this date.
This poses a real operational problem and risk, as deadlines are no longer set before the stock runs out, but after ((deadlines set after stock runs out)).

The few research indicate that it seems to be a behavior introduced with the upgrade; and system now include the calendar delay within the time fence and this is no longer broken by the heuristics in case of shortage .

Could you confirm this ' diagnosis ' and explanation according / attributing the change to the Upgrade ? Tx!

entry.title: Building a Production-Ready SAC Custom Widget with Claude AI — From Zero to Four Widgets
entry.content:
How I used Claude as an AI development partner to design and ship a full suite of Decomposition Tree story widgets for SAP Analytics Cloud — as a product manager with no coding background.

Why Custom Widgets Matter

SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP Business Data Cloud give you something genuinely powerful: a rich, governed analytics platform with native planning, integrated SAP data, and a polished story-building experience. Most visualisation needs are covered out of the box — but when teams require something more specialised, the Custom Widget SDK becomes the extension point.

Build any chart type using any open source visualisation library, package it, upload it to the platform, and it appears in the story widget panel just like any native chart. Full data binding. Full styling panel. The same configuration experience story designers already know.

Best of both worlds: the governed, integrated platform your organisation relies on, extended with exactly the visualisation your team needs.

This post is about how I built a suite of Decomposition Tree story widgets using that SDK — and how I used Claude as an AI development partner to make it happen as a product manager with no development background.

What Is a Decomposition Tree Widget?

A decomposition tree is a left-to-right hierarchical visualisation for variance analysis. Each node represents a dimension member — a product category, a region, a sales manager — and shows its contribution as a horizontal bar against a plan or target. Branches connect parent nodes to their children, coloured to immediately signal over- or under-performance.

What makes this particularly useful for planning scenarios is the combination of drill flexibility and at-a-glance variance signalling. A viewer can start at the total, expand only the categories that are off-plan, drill into brands within those categories, and leave everything else collapsed. The widget respects the viewer's mental model rather than forcing them to see everything at once.

SAP Analytics Cloud Custom Widgets — The Extension Point

The Custom Widget SDK is one of SAP Analytics Cloud's most powerful and underused capabilities. The idea is elegant: write a standard Web Component, describe its data bindings and configurable properties in a manifest file, package both as a zip, and upload to the platform. From that point on, the widget appears in the story widget panel alongside all native chart types.

Story designers bind it to data, configure its styling properties through a dedicated panel, and resize it like any other widget — all in the familiar story builder interface. The widget itself can use any JavaScript visualisation library: D3.js, ECharts, Chart.js, Highcharts, or anything else available from a CDN.

This is the best-of-both-worlds proposition: the governed, integrated SAP Analytics Cloud platform your organisation already relies on, with the freedom to deliver precisely the chart type your users need.

Getting Started: Requirements, Not Instructions

My starting point wasn't asking Claude to explain how custom widgets work. I didn't need to know. I described what I wanted — the visualisation, the features, the user experience — and gave Claude the reference material to work with: links to SAP's sample custom widget repository, relevant SAP Community blog posts, and the Custom Widget SDK documentation.

 
That was the pattern throughout the entire project. I stayed in product manager mode — describing outcomes, reacting to what I saw in SAP Analytics Cloud, and feeding Claude the context it needed. When something didn't look right, I'd paste in the error message or attach a screenshot. Claude would diagnose it and update the code.

Iterating Through Issues

Not everything worked first time — and when it didn't, the process was straightforward. Copy the error message from SAP Analytics Cloud, paste it into the conversation, attach a screenshot if it was a visual issue, and describe what I expected to see instead. Claude would identify the cause and produce an updated version of the relevant file.

Widget 1: The Foundation — Single Bar with Mini Charts

The first widget established the pattern every subsequent widget would build on. The goal: a horizontal bar per node coloured green or red by variance, with a dashed target tick, and mini bar charts showing the monthly breakdown inside each node.

Adding a Styling Panel

Once the core visualisation was working, I wanted story designers to be able to configure the widget's appearance directly in the story builder — colours, sizes, fonts, bar widths — without any code changes. I described what I needed: a panel that felt like the native chart styling options, with controls for every visual property, and dropdowns that reflected whichever dimensions the designer had bound to the widget.

The Custom Widget SDK supports exactly this through a dedicated styling panel — a separate component that appears alongside native chart styling options in the story builder. I gave Claude the requirements for each control and it built the panel, including the data binding that kept the dimension dropdowns in sync with whatever data was connected at the time. From a designer's perspective the result is seamless — configure the widget the same way you'd configure any native SAP Analytics Cloud chart.

The Widget Suite: An Iterative Journey

Each widget was built through conversation. I'd describe the user experience I wanted, Claude would generate the code and explain the key decisions, I'd validate it in SAP Analytics Cloud and refine the requirements based on what I saw, and we'd move to the next layer. Here's the progression:

 
Widgets 3 and 4: Refining the Experience
 
Widget 3 introduced the most requested feature: the ability to expand a single node without opening the entire level. Rather than clicking a category and seeing every brand underneath every other category expand at the same time, viewers could now explore just the one branch they cared about — everything else stayed as it was. It also introduced a dual-bar layout showing Actual and Plan as two separate bars per node for teams who wanted that explicit visual separation.
 
Widget 4 took the cleaner single-bar approach from Widget 1, added the per-node expand behaviour from Widget 3, and introduced one visual refinement I'd wanted from the start: branch lines that fade from the parent node's colour into the child node's colour. A small detail, but one that makes the variance story easier to read at a glance — you can trace a red thread through the hierarchy without having to read every label.
Both widgets went through the same process as Widget 1: describe the feature, validate in SAP Analytics Cloud, share what I saw, iterate. The conversation history from the earlier widgets meant Claude already understood the data model, the styling panel architecture, and the design decisions we'd already made — each new widget built directly on that shared context.

 

How I Actually Used Claude Day-to-Day

A few patterns emerged across the four widgets that made the collaboration work well:

1. Show Claude what you see, not what you think the fix is

When something didn't look or behave right, I didn't try to diagnose it myself — I just showed Claude what I was seeing. Sometimes that was a screenshot of the widget on the story canvas. Sometimes it was a console error I'd copied from the browser. Sometimes just a description: "the tooltip appears far below the cursor" or "clicking the expand button also opens the drill popup". Claude would identify the cause and update the code. As a non-developer, this is the approach that works: show the evidence, let Claude do the diagnosis.

2. Paste error messages exactly as they appear

Alongside screenshots, pasting the exact error text from SAP Analytics Cloud into the conversation was consistently the fastest path to a fix. The more precise the information — exact error text, a screenshot of the visual result, a description of what I expected instead — the faster we moved.

3. Build up a CLAUDE.md project knowledge file

As we confirmed what worked — the right packaging patterns, data binding conventions, rendering techniques — I asked Claude to help document each decision in a CLAUDE.md file in the project directory. By the end, this file captured every confirmed pattern, design choice, and platform behaviour we'd validated. Every new conversation started with Claude reading this context, so I never had to repeat or re-explain myself.

4. Incremental verification

I never asked Claude to write a full 800-line widget in one shot. We built it layer by layer: skeleton → data binding → tree layout → node rendering → styling panel → expand buttons. Each layer was tested in SAP Analytics Cloud before moving to the next.

5. Claude checks before you upload

Before packaging each new version, Claude would proactively review what it had built and flag anything worth addressing before testing in SAP Analytics Cloud — edge cases, timing issues, data states that only occur with certain model configurations, things that would only surface once the widget was live on a story canvas. I didn't have to ask.

Widget Suite at a Glance

You Don't Need to Know How It Works

One thing worth highlighting: I didn't need to understand the technical implementation to get here. The packaging format, the data binding conventions, the rendering patterns — Claude handled all of that. What I needed to understand was what I wanted the widget to do, how customers would want to use it, and what good looked like when I tested it in SAP Analytics Cloud.

If you're a fellow PM or business analyst wondering whether this is within reach — it is. The Custom Widget SDK is a developer tool, but with AI assistance you don't need to be the developer.

Conclusion

The widgets are running in our local tenants. Widget 4 (Decomposition Single Bar) is currently serving as the foundation for a production version our development team is building towards a beta release — the proof of concept gave us something concrete to react to and refine requirements against. Widget 3 (dual bar) remains available for use cases that benefit from the explicit visual separation of Actual and Plan.
 

One More Thing: Turning Everything We Learned into a Reusable Skill

By the end of this project, Claude had accumulated a deep working knowledge of the SAC Custom Widget SDK — every packaging rule, data binding quirk, cache busting step, and tooltip fix. That knowledge lived in a project file called CLAUDE.md that we built up iteratively throughout the work.
 
The natural next step was to distil that into a standalone Claude skill — a single file that captures everything needed to build a SAC custom widget from scratch. The idea is simple: instead of rediscovering the same gotchas on the next widget, you hand Claude the skill file at the start of a new project and it already knows everything. No trial and error, no re-explaining the packaging rules, no debugging the same cache issue twice.

 

The skill covers the full picture — index.json and main.js templates, data binding patterns, common errors and their fixes, the styling panel, linked analysis, and a plain-English guide for non-developers on what to tell Claude to get a working widget generated. Anyone can drop it into their own project and start building immediately.

It is something I am planning to share more broadly — both as a resource for others building on the Custom Widget SDK, and as part of the wider Custom Widget Marketplace initiative. If you would like a copy, reach out.

If you're considering building a custom widget for SAP Analytics Cloud, I hope this post gives you a practical picture of what's involved and what's achievable. The Custom Widget SDK is a genuinely powerful extension point — and with AI assistance, it's accessible even if you're coming at it from a product or business perspective rather than a development one.

 
Further reading
  • For the original SAP Custom Widget SDK guides, see series starting with: SAP Analytics Cloud Custom Widget – Hands-On Guide 
  • For more on AI-assisted SAP Analytics Cloud widget development, see the companion post by Adem Baykal: AI & Claude Code Experiment (Part 1): Building an SAP Analytics Cloud Widget

entry.title: Re: SAC: Scoping the date dimension using source version...
entry.content:
@Shailesh_mishra-983 

Currently you can only scope date based on version start and end property of a single version ( Target version parameter). So There are 2 option, either you use scripting to read the version attributes and assign the Data Action Parameter. Your User will have UX issues in this approach. The other way which it can work is you maintain an extra property say startdate2 in version dimension where the you keep start date of the source version as values this attribute startdate2  against Target member and then scope the date as below

MEMBERSET [d/Date] =[d/Version].[p/startdate2] TO [d/Version].[p/end]  // This will read property values of TargetVersion parameter

 

Hope this helps !

Nikhil

Nikhil

entry.title: Deleting records from replicated table
entry.content:
Hi

deleting data from a local table seems straight forward but can we delete data from a replicated table?

Scenario:
The source system is itself a replica of another using change data capture. Each morning it is purged (using a truncate) and repopulated with data that is only relevant to the current day (ie ignores anything in the source that was completed before the current day). This accumulates during the day.
So in Datasphere we have set up real-time replication.
It works well for newly inserted records, updates or deleted records that occur throughout the day.

The problem is that it does not delete all data that is purged overnight, no doubt because it doesn't capture the deletions that occur as a result of the truncate (rightly or wrongly, that's another debate). This means that DS holds more data than is necessary, and the volume increases rapidly over a few days

So what I would like to do is selectively delete any records from DS that would have been purged.

I know one alternative solution would be to change the CDC in the source from truncate to delete *, but that's not currently an option.

entry.title: Exploring AI-Driven Life Sciences Use Cases: Seeking Inputs from Experts
entry.content:
Reimagining Life Sciences with AI: A Call for Collaboration

In today’s rapidly evolving life sciences landscape, organizations are undergoing a significant transformation driven by data, automation, and Artificial Intelligence (AI). From accelerating innovation to optimizing operations, AI is emerging as a key enabler across pharma, biotech, and medical devices.
As part of the CIS Innovation Incubation team, we are working on building AI-driven use cases to enhance efficiency, improve decision-making, and deliver value to life sciences organizations, partners, and stakeholders.

Why Life Sciences?

The life sciences industry plays a critical role in advancing healthcare outcomes, spanning areas such as research and development, manufacturing, supply chain, quality management, and regulatory compliance. However, many processes still involve manual effort, fragmented systems, and delayed insights.
With the increasing volume of data-from research data to operational and regulatory systems-there is a strong opportunity for AI to:

  • Unlock actionable insights from complex datasets

  • Reduce manual and repetitive tasks

  • Improve speed and accuracy of decision-making

  • Enable more proactive and predictive operations

Our Objective

We are focusing on:

  • Identifying real-world pain points in life sciences processes

  • Understanding challenges faced by customers and partners

  • Co-creating impactful AI-driven solutions

Potential Areas of Innovation

Some of the broader areas where AI can create value in life sciences include:

  • Intelligent data analysis for faster insights

  • Predictive analytics for better planning and resource utilization

  • Automation of document-heavy and compliance-driven workflows

  • Conversational assistants for quick access to information

  • Real-time monitoring for risk, quality, and compliance

Collaboration Approach

To ensure meaningful impact, we are adopting a collaborative approach:

  • Engaging with Product Managers and domain experts

  • Conducting discussions to gather inputs and pain points

  • Running initial development in parallel for rapid prototyping

Call for Inputs

We would like to hear from the community:

  • What are the key challenges you face in life sciences today?

  • Which processes are time-consuming or error-prone?

  • Where do you see the highest potential for AI adoption?
    Your inputs will help us build meaningful and scalable solutions.

Next Steps

Based on the feedback, we will prioritize use cases, develop prototypes, and iterate with stakeholders.

Conclusion

AI has the potential to transform life sciences from reactive operations to proactive, data-driven decision-making. Collaboration will be key to achieving this transformation.

Looking forward to your inputs.

entry.title: SAC Data Blending issue
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

I am experiencing a data duplication issue when exporting
a blended data model from SAC Stories to Excel/CSV.

ENVIRONMENT:
- SAP Analytics Cloud (Stories)
- Live SuccessFactors EC connection
- Data Blending between 2 models
- Both models are flat compensation portlets

ISSUE:
When exporting blended table via widget Export,
rows are duplicated in multiples of 20 (x20, x40, etc)

PATTERN OBSERVED:
- Under 10,000 rows → export is clean, no duplication
- Over 20,000 rows → duplication starts, always x20 pattern
- SAC table/story displays correctly, issue only on export
- Duplication happens with composite key blend only
- Personnel Number only blend works fine at 80,000+ rows

COMPOSITE KEY FORMULA:
PersonnelNumber + PayComponentID +
YEAR + zero-padded MONTH + zero-padded DAY + Sequence

WHAT I HAVE TRIED:
- Added zero padding to month and day in key formula
- Verified no duplicates exist in source models individually
- Verified key uniqueness in both models
- Tested with simple flat portlets (no self-join) - same issue
- Widget export and general export both produce duplicates

CONCLUSION:
Issue is reproducible and consistent. Appears to be SAC
export pagination bug specific to composite key blending
on large datasets with live SuccessFactors connection.

Has anyone experienced this? Is there a known workaround
or SAP note addressing this?

Thank you

entry.title: SSAM Offline OData: Parent Entity Not Deleted When Child Entity is Local (@sap.isLocal = true)
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I would like to clarify the behaviour of Offline OData in SAP Service and Asset Manager (SSAM) regarding deletion when dependent entities are still in the local state.

Scenario

We have the following hierarchy:

  • WorkOrder (Primary Entity)
    → Operation
    → MobileStatus

Current situation:

  • MobileStatus has @sap.isLocal = true
  • This indicates the entity is local on the client (changes sent but not yet confirmed via download)
  • Backend sends a DELETE response for WorkOrder and Operation

Understanding Based on SAP Documentation

From SAP Offline OData documentation:

1. Local & Pending State
  • Local entities exist in the offline store
  • Pending changes are stored in the request queue
  • An entity can be local but not pending (i.e., sent to backend, response not yet received)
2. Sync Behaviour
  • The application operates in the offline store
  • Backend changes are applied via a refresh of the existing store, not overwritten
3. Conflict Handling
  • Server responses are applied after considering the local state
  • Local changes may take precedence until resolved
4. Data Consistency
  • Offline OData maintains referential integrity
  • Prevents deletion of the parent if a dependent child exists locally

 

Observation

Even though the backend sends a delete for:

  • WorkOrder
  • Operation

The entities are still visible on the client

 

My Understanding

Based on the above, my understanding is:

  • Local entities (@sap.isLocal = true) are still part of the offline store state
  • Backend responses are applied considering existing local data
  • If a dependent child entity exists locally,
    → Parent deletion may not be applied to maintain consistency

 

Question

Can you please confirm if the following is correct:

If a dependent child entity (e.g., MobileStatus) is still in local state,
The Offline OData store will not apply backend delete of parent entities
until the local state is fully resolved (synced)?

 

Goal

We want to confirm whether this behaviour is:

  • Standard Offline OData design, OR
  • Something specific to configuration or data state

 

 

Any clarification or references would be really helpful.

Thanks in advance!

entry.title: 1H 2026 SmartRecruiters for SAP SuccessFactors: Connected Solutions and New AI-First Capabilities
entry.content:
With the 1H 2026 release, we have delivered new capabilities within SmartRecruiters for SAP SuccessFactors to improve how organizations intelligently attract and hire the best talent.  

To learn more watch the on-demand Q&A webinars: 1H 2026 Product Release Webinars and Recruiting & Onboarding 1H 2026 Customer Release Q&A. To hear more about the roadmap and broader view of our strategy, watch the 1H 2026 VIP Virtual Program for Strategic Roadmap Updates.    

All of the SAP SuccessFactors 1H 2026 release items can be found on the What’s New Viewer. These new features will be live in Production on May 15th, 2026.

With the 1H 2026 release, we have delivered a deeper integration between SAP SuccessFactors HCM and SmartRecruiters for SAP SuccessFactors as well as updates to Winston, SmartRecruiters’ suite of AI products, which are now available for SAP SuccessFactors customers.

Our integration with SmartRecruiters allows for more consistency and synergy across both products and allows our customers to take advantage of the latest recruiting AI in candidate conversion and team engagement.

Now let’s explore the new features, including a deeper integration between SAP SuccessFactors and SmartRecruiters as well as updates to Winston products such as Companion, Chat, and Match.

SmartRecruiters & SAP SuccessFactors Integration

SmartRecruiters and SAP SuccessFactors now have a native integration with HCM sync and SSO login. That means a seamless, unified experience across both ecosystems with easy connection to Employee Central and Onboarding modules.

With these solutions connected, you can look forward to greater operational efficiency that eliminates the need to manually enter data or replicate work.

SmartRecruiters Winston for SAP SuccessFactors Launch

SmartRecruiters Winston for SAP SuccessFactors is now fully available for SAP SuccessFactors Recruiting customers, bringing conversational AI capabilities into your current solution to elevate candidate engagement and experiences.

SmartRecruiters Winston for SAP SuccessFactors runs on your career site to help recruiting teams engage candidates at scale with generative AI, from job discovery and applications to screening and interview scheduling.

The entire candidate journey is delivered conversationally, so every action is as easy as sending a message to a friend. For example, a candidate looking for a job could simply ask Winston ‘do you have any sales jobs in Seattle?’ and receive an answer directly in the chat.

Look forward to boosting candidate conversion and reducing administrative work with these feature highlights:

  • Chat available 24/7
  • Customizable avatar, colors, and tone of voice
  • Conversational job discovery, applications, and screening questions
  • Automated interview scheduling based on criteria like answers to screening questions.
  • Dashboard to review active bots and performance metrics

Winston Companion: Q&A Agent

Delivered within the core SmartRecruiters for SAP SuccessFactors solution, customers benefit from Winston’s enhanced AI capabilities that streamline recruiting processes and drive better business outcomes. Winston Companion’s Q&A agent is your hiring team’s one-stop shop for instant, trustworthy recruiting system and process answers. Users can ask questions without leaving their workflow, rather than opening new tabs and manually looking through article after article.

This convenient new feature helps recruiters save time and stay on task with the following feature highlights:

  • Ask questions about the recruiting process or system directly in Winston Companion.
  • Get instant, contextualized answers with link references to ensure accuracy and allow you to dive deeper.

Winston Companion: Auto Self-Scheduling

Winston Companion’s Auto Self-Scheduling gives hirers a fast, low‑touch way to move qualified candidates from shortlist to confirmed interviews, without the usual back‑and‑forth over email or chat.

By combining preconfigured interview templates, calendar availability, and automation rules, Winston Companion can trigger one‑click scheduling flows directly from a conversational prompt, whether a hirer is working in the web app or via mobile channels like WhatsApp.

Look forward to increased efficiency with the following key features:

  • Template‑driven automation that uses existing interview templates and self-scheduling rules to send out accurate, consistent invites
  • Bulk invitations for high‑volume roles

Winston Match: Subscores

Winston Match subscores give recruiters a holistic, nuanced view of how each candidate’s skills, experience, and education contribute to their overall Match Score. Instead of a single aggregate number, subscores expose the underlying signals Winston uses to evaluate fit, so teams can see not just who is ranked highly, but why they’re ranked that way.

For high-volume screening, subscores make Winston Match insights instantly usable in list views. This accelerates shortlisting while keeping recruiters firmly in control of who moves forward through the following key features.

  • Subscore dimensions of skills, experience, and education/certifications
  • Easy overview of subscores in the applicant list
  • Flexible UI allows users to hide the subscore column if not needed

SmartRecruiters Winston for SAP SuccessFactors: Search Jobs via Resume with Winston Chat

SmartRecruiters Winston for SAP SuccessFactors: Search Jobs via Resume makes job discovery as simple as dropping a CV into the conversation through Winston Chat. Instead of typing keywords or scrolling through pages of listings, candidates upload their resume and let Winston Chat surface relevant roles automatically.

Enable recruiters to reduce friction in the application process while also matching candidates to the most relevant roles, so you can look forward to a boost in high-quality candidate conversion.

Continuous Innovation

At SAP, our goal with each release is to make the recruiting process more efficient and consistent while adding a touch of joy along the way. We want to empower hirers to take time for strategic tasks and meaningful communication that makes your team more visible and valued.

That’s why we are so excited about this round of updates. With the enhanced suite of Winston capabilities, recruiters can leverage generative AI directly within their workflow to boost the metrics that matter to them, like candidate conversion and time to hire, whether that means leveraging Winston Match subscores or SmartRecruiters Winston for SAP SuccessFactors automated scheduling.

With every release, we are excited to deliver these new innovations and continue our journey of providing even greater value to our customers. For full details, please check out the 1H 2026 Release Highlights Resources: 

  • 1H 2026 Release Highlights Brochure
  • 1H 2026 Release Highlights Video 
  • Product Release & Road Map Information

Learn more about SmartRecruiters for SAP SuccessFactors.

entry.title: SAP Ariba Supplier Management & Risk 2605 Release Highlights
entry.content:
Hello SAP Ariba Supplier Management & Risk (SM&R) Community!

If you're interested in learning about some of the new features coming with our May release, you're in the right place. 

Let's take a look at what's new within SM&R this quarter...

 

SAP Ariba Supplier Lifecycle and Performance

Additional Authorizations for AI Copilot Joule Scenarios in Supplier Management (SLP-2412)

This feature refines the requirements for user group membership to see prompts for specific Joule supplier management scenarios. This refinement ensures that prompts related to specific aspects of supplier management functionality are only visible  to users with relevant permissions, in customer landscapes that include the applicable solutions.

 

 

 

Ability to Restrict the Number of Commodity, Region, and Department Answers in External Questionnaires (SLP-2382)

This feature enables you to configure the maximum number of answers process initiators can add to Commodity, Region and Department questions in the external modular questionnaire.

Process initiators can only add the maximum number of answers you specify when creating a process, ensuring that processes meet your business requirements. It is applicable for external modular questionnaire. If the data is pre-populated from the registration stage or initial values specified from the template, the selected items need to be within the defined levels in the template configuration. If the pre-populated answers exceed the specified range, you must remove the values that are outside of the defined levels in the template configuration. 

 

 

SAP Ariba Supplier Risk

Additional Joule Capabilities in SAP Ariba Supplier Risk (SRISK-1860)

Your AI copilot Joule integrates with the Risk Exposure API - making it that much faster to find information via natural language prompting.

Take a look at these examples: 

 

Enhanced Virus scanning capability in Supplier Risk (SRISK-1329)

This feature automatically scans all files uploaded during different stages of an engagement request for viruses and malware, providing enhanced security protection throughout the supplier onboarding process. When documents are uploaded during any stage of creating an engagement request, each file is scanned in real-time before the next upload can begin. This sequential upload process ensures thorough security screening of documents.

Previously, you could upload multiple files consecutively without interruption. The system now requires a scan to complete before you can upload the next file. Please allow a brief moment between file uploads.

 

 

 

Enhancing Risk Exposure API by Adding Standard and Custom Category in Response (SRISK-1856)

 This feature enables the Risk Exposure API to retrieve responses for both standard and custom risk categories for suppliers you monitor in SAP Ariba Supplier Risk. The responses depend on the parameter setting for Enable custom risk categories (Application.SR.Risk.EnableCustomRiskCategories) on your site.

The current version (v3) of Risk Exposure API returns responses regardless of the parameter setting. 

When the Enable custom risk categories parameter is enabled, the API returns data for your custom risk categories.

When the parameter is disabled, the API automatically returns data for the default risk categories: Environmental & Social, Financial, Operational, and Regulatory & Legal.

(The previous version (v2) of the Risk Exposure API returned responses only when the Enable custom risk categories parameter was enabled.)

 

Once these features are officially released (May 15th) you can locate additional implementation and technical details, by simply searching for the feature ID here: What's New (via SAP Help Portal)

 

 

​Stay tuned for our additional upcoming blog posts and follow me to stay informed on upcoming feature releases across SAP Ariba.

​​Thank you for being a part of the SAP Ariba community and we look forward to continuing our journey of innovation together!

 

 

​​

 

Resources:

What’s New - where you can find implementation and Technical Details for any of these features (once released on May 15)

SAP Ariba Connect - Release Readiness (Customers except CIM)

Readiness Community page (For CIM customers)

SAP Community for Intelligent Spend Management page

SAP Help Portal pages - Procurement and Network

entry.title: Re: Tyre Tracking Requirement – Design Confirmation & Performance
entry.content: Treating each tyre as serialized equipment is a common approach for full lifecycle tracking, especially when you need history like install/dismantle and position changes. From a design perspective, it makes sense. That said, with ~100k objects per year, it’s definitely worth validating performance and data volume impact in the public cloud. I’d recommend discussing it with DAP early, just to be safe.

entry.title: First Half 2026 Release: What's New for SAP SuccessFactors Career and Talent Development
entry.content:
In the 1H 2026 release, we continue to focus on how organizations plan, develop, and grow their talent. AI agents and new experiences support how talent foundations help organizations develop the skills and capabilities needed for future growth.

We’re excited to announce the Career Development Agent, now available to make employee growth easier with AI-driven insights. It offers a conversational way for employees to get career questions answered and helps close skill gaps faster by automatically creating personalized development plans tailored to target roles. By making career paths more transparent and aligned with employees’ current skills and traditional role progressions it guides meaningful growth. Identifying viable next roles and clearly outlining the capabilities needed to advance gives employees confidence to move forward while enabling organizations to drive talent mobility.
👀  Check out this demo to see the agent in action: Demo: Career Development Agent 

Also with this release, we are introducing the Succession Planning Agent, which transforms succession by continuously evaluating the health of succession plans and offering clear, actionable recommendations to secure talent pipelines reducing manual work for all succession planners. It helps to proactively mitigate succession risks and make data informed decisions faster, supporting retention and engaging top talent while optimizing where succession resources are allocated. The result is a stronger, more resilient talent pipeline that keeps organizations prepared for change.
🌟  Take a look at the demo to learn more: Demo: Succession Planning Agent 

We are further enhancing the succession experience with deeper EU-aligned compliance, enhanced consent management, and improved filtering. Administrators can now use role-based permissions to control the visibility of overall performance ratings in AI-assisted successor insights, preventing sensitive information from being exposed to unauthorized users. Also, generic object and foundation object filters are now supported in Position Tile live slides allowing users to efficiently filter and present positions in Presentations.

Additionally, enhanced personalized recommendations leverages advanced AI for learning, roles, and career paths, providing consistent, personalized growth at scale. Automatically generated suggestions adapt by career level, location, skills, interests, proficiency, and organizational priorities, helping to ensure recommendations align with company rules and goals. Employees receive skill-focused learning suggestions that drive meaningful development and a more relevant, user-friendly experience to support career progression and build critical capabilities across the organization.

Explore more innovations across the suite in the 1H 2026 release by checking out the release highlights brochure, overview video featuring Bianka Woelke and much more on the Product Release & Road Map Information page.

Learn more about SAP SuccessFactors Career and Talent Development

entry.title: SAP Developer News for April 23rd, 2026
entry.content:
ITEMS 

Migration tool for your SAP Build Apps frontend web applications 

  • Blog post https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/migration-tool-for-your-sap-build-apps-frontend-web-applications/ba-p/14368805  
  • SAP Help page https://help.sap.com/docs/build-apps/service-guide/sap-build-apps-path-forward#migrating-from-the-sap-build-apps-standalone-offering-to-sap-build 

SAP Open Source Webinar: Olympic Sailing on Eclipse Technology – From Closed Source to Open Source 

  • Event info https://events.sap.com/olympic-sailing-on-eclipse-technology-from-closed-source-to-open/en_us/home.html  

SAP BTP Platform Automation Roundtable #3 

  • Blog post https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/customer-amp-partner-roundtable-for-sap-btp-platform-automation-3-april/ba-p/14374109  

DYK #13 Override default auth while using xsuaa (CAP) 

  • Capire: Authentication in Production – Enforced by Default https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/node.js/authentication#enforced-by-default  

CHAPTER TITLES 

00:00 Intro 
00:07 Migration tool for your SAP Build Apps frontend web applications 
01:31 SAP Open Source Webinar: Olympic Sailing on Eclipse Technology – From Closed Source to Open Source 
02:24 SAP BTP Platform Automation Roundtable #3 
03:24 DYK #13 Override default auth while using xsuaa (CAP) 

TRANSCRIPT

[Intro]
This is the SAP Developer News for April the 23rd, 2026.

[Shrini]
Hello, SAP community. By now, you may have seen the announcement about SAP Build Apps deprecation as of March 2026. Understandably, the first question on everyone's mind is, what happens to the apps I am already building? The team has been listening closely to the feedback from the community. To make this transition as seamless as possible, SAP has rolled out a migration tool tool for your SAP Build Apps projects. This tool will transform your existing front-end web applications into a fully structured React or SAP UI5 project. Once transformed, you can easily continue enhancing them within your SAP Build environment using Business Application Studio. If you are ready to migrate, you can reach out to SAP by opening a support ticket with the mtar file of your project, specify whether the app should be based on React or SAPUI5 and authorize the transformation. Detailed instructions on creating this ticket are available on this help page. The link can be found in the description. This tool was built based on your feedback. Let us know how this works for you. Finally, a quick reminder, you will continue to have full access to SAP BuildApps for the entire duration of your current contract.

[Mamikee]
Sailing Analytics is now open source, and if you want to learn more about the journey behind it, there's an upcoming webinar you don't want to miss. On May 20th, 2026 at 3pm CEST, join the technical webinar on Eclipse Azimuth Sailing Analytics, a real-time regatta tracking and analysis tool that has been used at more than 1,000 sailing events, including the Olympic Games. In the session, product owner Axel will walk through SAP's journey to open source the solution, including licensing strategy, internal processing, collaboration with the Eclipse Foundation, and community building efforts. Now, if you're interested in open source, real-world analytics solutions, or how projects like this evolve beyond SAP, this is definitely one to check out. You can learn more and register in the link below.

[DJ]
Are you interested in the Terraform and CLI tools for managing your BTP resources? Sharing opinions and experiences on this topic with others? Then the BTP Platform Automation Customer and Partner Roundtable series is for you. And the third instance of the series is just around the corner. It's on Tuesday, the 12th of May at As usual, there will be some info on latest updates, a round of Q&A, some demos and more. There's a blog
post with further details, a link that you can use to register. It's free, by the way. And also in that blog post, there's a link to content from the previous two instances. See you there.

[Shrini]
Did you know you can override the default authentication behavior for CAP service endpoints using a simple feature flag? By default, when you deploy a CAP service to Cloud Foundry with a production -grade authentication strategy, all service endpoints are protected. This happens because CAP implicitly adds the @requires authenticated user annotation to every service. But what if you want to make some endpoints publicly accessible, especially during testing or for specific use cases? You can disable this default behavior by adding the flag RestrictAllServicesToFalse to your package.json. In this batch request service, setting the RestrictAllServices flag to false makes all service endpoints publicly accessible by default, meaning no authentication is required. However, the createBatch action is explicitly protected using the @RequiresAuthenticatedUser annotation, ensuring that only authenticated users can invoke it.

entry.title: Automating Probation Extensions After Sick Leave in SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central
entry.content:
Introduction

In many organizations, the probation period is designed to give both the employer and the employee a fair window to assess performance, cultural fit, and overall suitability for the role. But what happens when that evaluation period is interrupted? Employee absences, especially due to short term sickness, can significantly reduce the time available for meaningful observation, leaving managers with incomplete information at the end of the probation timeline.

To address this, some companies adopt a policy of extending the probation period by the exact number of days an employee was absent during that time. While the intent is straightforward, the execution can quickly become complex. Tracking absence days, recalculating end dates, and ensuring consistency across the organization often leads to manual effort, errors, and compliance risks.

This is where SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central (EC) can play a crucial role. By leveraging automation and business rules, organizations can seamlessly adjust probation end dates based on recorded absences, ensuring fairness, accuracy, and operational efficiency. In this blog, we’ll explore how to design and implement an automated approach to extending probation periods when short term sick leave occurs, helping HR teams move from manual tracking to a more intelligent, system-driven process.

Proposed Solution

The proposed solution is based on standard SuccessFactors functionalities, namely Intelligent Services Center (ISC), Integration Center (IC), and Business Rules.

Below is a step-by-step guide to building it.

Step 1: Navigate to Integration Center and click “My Integrations” to start creating your IC job.

Step 2: Now click on “+ Create” and select “More Integration Types”.

Step 3: In this step, you need to define the integration type. For this scenario, select the options as shown in the screenshot below. Once selected, click “Create.”

Step 4: On the first screen of the IC job, you are required to provide a name, a description, and a page size. Below is an example from a demo system. Once these fields are defined, click “Next.”

Step 5: Next, navigate to the “Configure Fields” tab and, from the menu in the top-right corner, select the “Switch to Field Mapping View” option.

On this page, we first define the destination table, for this scenario, we use “Job Information.”

Next, we configure the mapping from the “Employee Time” table to Job Information. As shown in the screenshot below, only two fields are directly mapped: “Event Date,” which is mapped to the absence “End Date,” and “User ID,” mapped to the “User ID” from the source table.

These mappings are created easily by dragging and dropping fields from the source table on the left to the corresponding fields in the destination table on the right.

Step 6: Next, switch to “Detail View” using the option in the top-right menu.

We will now define fields that do not have a direct mapping but instead require a default value. To do this, locate the “Sequence Number” field in the table on the right. Select it, then in the left panel assign a value greater than 2, for example, 9.

Setting a number higher than 2 helps prevent potential errors. If, on the same day, the job attempts to create a new time slice in Job Information while another record already exists, the system will use this higher value to generate an additional sequence number. It’s important to note that the final sequence may not necessarily be 9, as the system intelligently determines the correct sequence based on existing records.

Now we complete the second action in this step by assigning a default value to the “Event Reason” field for this scenario.

Note: It is recommended to create a dedicated event reason for this use case to avoid unintentionally triggering the business rule (which we will configure later) in other situations.

Step 7: We have now completed the creation of our IC job, so click “Save” as shown in the screenshot.

Step 8: In this step, we will assign the IC job we created to the ISC. Navigate to the Intelligent Services Center page, search for the event “Employee Short Term Disability,” and click to open it.

Step 9: Create a new flow if one already exists for this event. Click “Integration” from the right-hand menu, which will add a new line in the center of the page. Set the “Timing” to “When event is published.” Then, use the three-dot menu (highlighted in green) to save your changes.

Note: It is also advisable to create an ISC rule that limits the process to employees whose probation status is neither completed nor failed (or based on any other criteria relevant to your organization).

Step 10: In this step, we will create our first business rule, which will trigger the Intelligent Services Center event. To do this, navigate to the “Configure Object Definition” transaction and select the “Employee Time” object.

Click to create a new rule, following the example below. Once the rule is created, assign it on Save in the object and then save the object.

Note: The absence type used in the rule example is for demonstration purposes only. Please ensure you use the absence type relevant to your organization.

Step 11: Our final step is to create a business rule that calculates the new probation end date considering the number of days the employee was on sick leave.

To do this, navigate to “Manage Business Configuration” > “Job Information.” In edit mode, scroll to the bottom of the page. On the last line, click to create a new business rule as shown below. Once the rule is created, assign it to the onSave event in the portlet and save your changes.

Conclusion

By combining Intelligent Services Center (ISC), Integration Center (IC), and Business Rules, we have demonstrated how SAP SuccessFactors can be configured to automate the extension of probation periods based on employee sick leave. This approach ensures that any absence occurring during the probation period is accurately reflected by extending the end date accordingly, eliminating the need for manual adjustments.

Beyond improving administrative efficiency, this solution also enhances fairness and consistency in HR processes, ensuring that all employees are evaluated over an equivalent active working period. It reduces the risk of human error, strengthens compliance with internal policies, and provides a scalable framework that can be adapted to other absence-driven scenarios.

Finally, this use case illustrates the power of SAP SuccessFactors standard tools when combined thoughtfully. With the right configuration, organizations can transform complex HR requirements into automated, transparent, and maintainable processes that support both HR teams and employees alike.

I hope this solution helps you improve your Employee Central setup. Feel free to reach out or comment if you have questions, or if you’d like to share how you’ve tackled probation extensions due to employees absences in your own environment.

entry.title: Re: Compatibility Pack: How to check it in your system going forward?
entry.content:
If your production system has a large amount of ST03 usage data for the 3 month evaluation period, the following issue could be affecting you.  This was found with two of our larger ERP systems.

In the application log after executing the check, we saw many entries with the following note: "Relevance: MISS_ST03N - To determine relevance automatically, there need to be at least 10 sets of usage data in transaction ST03N. "  So we were not getting accurate results.

After debugging the program, I found a check that stops collecting usage data after 1.5 million records which makes no sense.  After working thru a Case with SAP, I was told to comment out the check and SAP would come up with a fix in a future release.  No OSS note yet.  But with May 31 looming...I thought I would post about it now.

entry.title: Re: Workflow delegation CC roles
entry.content:
Delegation in EC is designed only for actionable workflow steps (approvals).

CC roles are not part of any workflow step—they are only notification recipients. Since there is no action (approve/decline) associated with them, they are not considered in delegation logic.

That’s why notifications for CC roles cannot be redirected via delegation. If someone needs to receive notifications, they must be explicitly maintained in the workflow (CC or dynamic role).

Hope this answers your query.

entry.title: Impacts of SAP API Policy v4/2026 on existing customer integrations
entry.content:
With the release of SAP API Policy v4/2026, SAP reinforces that only published APIs (as listed in SAP Business Accelerator Hub or official product documentation) can be consumed, and explicitly disallows the use of internal, private, or non-published APIs — regardless of whether the system is Public Cloud, Private Cloud, or accessed via SAP BTP.

Many customers have long-running integrations and custom architectures (including SAP BTP-based extensions) that technically work today but rely on internal or non-released APIs.

My questions to the community are:

  1. How is SAP positioning the impact of this policy on existing customer architectures that are currently non-compliant?
  2. Is SAP recommending a concrete transition strategy or grace period, or should customers proactively plan migration to published APIs?
  3. In practice, how should customers prioritize remediation for productive integrations that rely on non-published APIs?

I am particularly interested in real-life experiences, guidance from SAP, or best practices adopted by customers and partners since this policy was updated.

Link to the Policy: SAP API Policy
